title: Dog Abdominal Heaving to Breathe: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog struggling to breathe with visible stomach movement? This is a high-priority emergency. Learn what to do and how to help your pet now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Abdominal heaving is a life-threatening veterinary emergency where a dog struggles to breathe; seek immediate emergency veterinary care as this can lead to rapid respiratory arrest.

What is abdominal heaving and why is my dog breathing with their stomach?

Abdominal heaving occurs when a dog is using their stomach muscles to assist in the process of breathing, a condition technically known as dyspnea. Under normal circumstances, breathing is effortless and involves the chest. When you see the abdomen noticeably pumping or sinking in and out with every breath, it indicates that your dog is struggling to move enough oxygen into their lungs or carbon dioxide out.

Is abdominal heaving in dogs a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Yes, abdominal heaving is a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate intervention.
  • Difficulty breathing means your dog's vital organs are not receiving the oxygen they need to function, which can lead to rapid decline.
  • Failure to act quickly can result in respiratory arrest.
  • Stop reading and transport your dog to the nearest open veterinary clinic or emergency hospital immediately.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to improve on their own.

How does providing a video of my dog's breathing help the emergency team?

  • If you have a passenger who can safely record a 5-to-10-second video of your dog’s breathing during transport, it provides vital information for the medical team.
  • A video allows the triage nurse to evaluate the specific rhythm and effort of the breathing from a distance.
  • This allows the staff to categorize the severity of the case and prepare oxygen therapy or stabilization equipment before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in dogs manifests as increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by changes in respiratory pattern, and postural changes such as standing with elbows abducted, back arched, or high on the rear haunches with head and neck extended. Labored, open-mouth breathing and cyanosis develop later, indicating significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. Observation of the breathing pattern and thoracic auscultation can help differentiate between pleural space and parenchymal disease. Pleural space disease causes asynchronous breathing, where the chest expands on inspiration as the abdomen is pulled inward, then the chest moves inward on expiration as the abdomen expands. Lung parenchymal disease causes quiet, smooth breathing where the chest and abdominal wall move in the same direction. Taking radiographs or performing stressful diagnostic procedures before stabilization can lead to rapid decompensation.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: What are other signs of respiratory distress in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include blue or purple-tinged gums, an extended neck, flared nostrils, and a look of panic or inability to get comfortable.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human asthma medication or oxygen?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications or attempt home treatments for breathing issues, as these can be fatal. Immediate professional veterinary care is the only safe option.

faqs.question: Should I call the vet before I arrive?
faqs.answer: Yes. If possible, have someone call the clinic while you are en route. This allows the staff to clear a space and have a medical team standing by for your arrival.
